professor.name: Dr. Okafor
professor.text: We've been discussing how schools handle homework this semester. A growing number of districts have started banning homework for elementary students entirely, arguing that young children need free time to play and that nightly assignments mostly create stress for families. Some educators believe this protects childhood and reduces inequality, since not every kid has a quiet place or help at home, while others worry that students will fall behind without daily practice and won't build study habits early enough. So, do you think eliminating homework in the early grades is a good idea? Why or why not?

students.name: Lena
students.text: Honestly, I think scrapping it is the right call for young kids. At that age, repetition in class matters far more than worksheets done at the kitchen table, where the quality depends entirely on the parents. Removing homework levels the field for students whose families can't sit down with them every night.

students.name: Marco
students.text: I see the appeal, but I think a small amount of daily practice is genuinely valuable. Skills like reading and basic arithmetic fade fast without consistent reinforcement, and ten minutes of homework also teaches kids to manage responsibilities. The fix is making assignments lighter, not abolishing them outright.

professor.name: Dr. Reyes
professor.text: For this week's discussion, let's think about how some countries are experimenting with a four-day workweek without cutting pay. Trial programs in places like Iceland and several UK firms reported that productivity held steady or even rose, while employees reported far less burnout. Some economists argue this is the future of efficient labor, since rested workers waste less time, while others caution that the gains may not transfer to factories, hospitals, or customer-facing jobs where output is tied directly to hours. Would you support shifting to a four-day workweek across the broader economy?

students.name: Aisha
students.text: I'm genuinely in favor of it. The evidence suggests much of a typical office day is consumed by unfocused meetings and busywork, so compressing the week pressures everyone to prioritize what actually matters. A more rested workforce is also a more sustainable one over the long term.

students.name: Eric
students.text: I'm skeptical that it scales beyond knowledge work. In sectors like nursing or manufacturing, output is fundamentally proportional to staffed hours, so a shorter week just means hiring more people or covering fewer shifts. Those added costs would inevitably get passed on to consumers.

professor.name: Dr. Novak
professor.text: We've been examining how streaming services compete for attention this semester. Several platforms have quietly started auto-playing the next episode within seconds, removing the natural pause where a viewer might decide to stop. Some media analysts defend this as a convenience that simply matches what audiences want, while others argue it's a deliberate design choice engineered to override self-control and inflate watch time. What's your perspective? Should platforms be required to make auto-play something users opt into rather than the default?

students.name: Yuki
students.text: I think defaulting it on is manipulative and should change. Most people never touch their settings, so the platform is effectively making the decision to keep them watching for them. Requiring an explicit opt-in respects the viewer's autonomy without taking the feature away from anyone who wants it.

students.name: Cameron
students.text: To be fair, I don't see this as the platform's responsibility to police. Adults can pause or close the app whenever they choose, and treating viewers as helpless against a single button feels condescending. The convenience is real, and burying it behind a toggle would frustrate the majority who actually like it.

professor.name: Dr. Hassan
professor.text: We've been studying how cities try to curb unhealthy diets this semester. A few municipalities have begun requiring chain restaurants to print not just calories but the equivalent amount of exercise needed to burn off each menu item, like '45 minutes of walking.' Some public health researchers argue these vivid labels nudge people toward better choices more effectively than abstract numbers, while others contend they shame diners, ignore deeper economic causes of poor diets, and may worsen disordered eating. Do you think exercise-based labeling is a worthwhile policy?

students.name: Phoebe
students.text: I think it's a clever and worthwhile nudge. A calorie count is meaningless to most people, but framing a dessert as an hour of jogging makes the consequence concrete and immediate. That kind of tangible translation is exactly what helps someone pause before ordering.

students.name: Jared
students.text: I'm wary of this approach because it reduces a complex problem to individual guilt. Poor diets are largely driven by cost and limited access to fresh food, not a lack of awareness, and these labels do nothing about that. For people prone to disordered eating, they could be actively harmful.
